summary: Stancil argues that the SaaS delivery model incentivizes shipping incomplete products, with customers effectively doing QA, and that this pattern has bled into hardware via cloud-dependent AI devices. He suggests this creates customer fatigue and financial strain for SaaS firms, opening space for a countercultural model: software sold as finished, one-time-purchase products. He then pivots to a related puzzle—why software hasn’t become a luxury status symbol despite its craftsmanship and ubiquity.
money_quotes: Vendors ship incomplete software, the threshold for what makes a bad release slips a bit further, and the whole thing spirals in a slow devolutionary loop, until people are blaming reviewers for not liking useless garbage, because of some open-ended promise that it might one day not be useless.
Build something that you don’t have to keep building forever; sell the product based on what it is today and not based on what you can promise it’ll be in the future.
Ultimately, that’s the interesting question here: Will someone pull this off? Will someone build a big software company that isn’t a SaaS product?
Put differently, on this last point, why isn’t there status software?
Because hope isn’t optimism. Hope is a thing with features.
key_insight: The SaaS model’s incentives to ship unfinished software may be unsustainable, creating an opening for non‑SaaS products and even new forms of software as status goods.
